answer: Argon2id with 64 MB memory, 3 iterations, and parallelism 4.
Refresh tokens expire in 7 days and are rotated on every use.
PostgreSQL 15.4, upgraded from 14.9.
API Gateway throttle is 5000 burst and 2000 steady.
Argon2id was chosen over bcrypt for stronger side-channel resistance.
Drizzle is used instead of Prisma for zero runtime dependencies and SQL-like TypeScript.
Both services validate JWTs via JWKS (`/.well-known/jwks.json`) under the all-services-auth rule.
Rate limiting is done at ALB to stop abusive traffic before containers (earlier DDoS mitigation than app-level limits).

Chain: API Gateway schema validation -> Lambda authorizer JWT validation via JWKS with claims injection -> VPC Link -> ALB per-IP limit -> Portfolio Service JWKS validation -> RBAC/role hierarchy check before rebalance.

If JWKS is down, downstream auth validation fails in Gateway Lambda and all services, while token issuance continues because the private key is local. A bounded LRU cache (1000 entries, 5-minute TTL) provides grace for cached keys.
Real-time portfolio alerts are planned for Q3 and require BOTH Notification Service work and Gateway WebSocket API work; status is planned, not active.
Yes. The UTC+13/UTC+14 expiry bug is resolved by using Unix timestamps instead of Date objects.

Use the existing webhook channel in Notification Service (register via `POST /api/v1/notifications/webhooks/register`) and deliver auth events with the platform retry policy (exponential backoff, 5 attempts/24h). Auth-event payload/signature contract details are NOT IN CONTEXT.
100 simultaneous DB connections maximum (20 per instance x 5 instances).
The DB pool cap (20/instance) limits connection concurrency, but the Celery memory growth during large portfolio/tax-lot recalcs is a separate unbounded-memory issue under investigation.
No. Fargate Spot is only for batch jobs (portfolio-recalc, digest), not always-on services like Auth due to interruption risk.
The stateless JWT/JWKS model (multi-service verification without shared server-side session state) argues against an in-memory Auth session cache.
30-day backups define retention/recovery window, while DR targets are RPO 1h and RTO 4h via us-west-2 replication; backups complement those DR objectives.
After the RDS upgrade, DB connections require `sslmode=verify-full` and `global-bundle.pem`.
`tax_lots` columns: id, account_id, security_id, acquisition_date, cost_basis, quantity, adjusted, created_at.
MFA adds a required step after password verification: if enabled, login must pass TOTP (or valid recovery code) before token issuance.
10 recovery codes are generated; each is single-use and permanently consumed when used.
Webhook retries use exponential backoff with 5 attempts over 24 hours.

Credential/security posture: passwords hashed with Argon2id (64MB/3 iterations/parallelism 4); PII encrypted at rest with AES-256-GCM per-tenant keys; transit protected by AppMesh/Envoy mTLS; PostgreSQL TLS uses `sslmode=verify-full` plus `global-bundle.pem`; JWTs are RS256 with JWKS validation; secrets rotate every 90 days.

With MFA enabled: request passes Gateway schema validation, traverses VPC Link to ALB (1000/min/IP), Auth `handlePostLogin` verifies Argon2id password, MFA challenge is completed via TOTP or recovery code, then Auth issues RS256 access token (15 min) plus refresh token (7 days, rotate-on-use).

Billing Service should follow platform patterns: validate JWTs via JWKS (and trust Gateway claims flow), use RFC7807 errors with traceId, structured JSON logging with request IDs, real-Postgres testcontainers tests (no mocked DB), and deploy through GitHub Actions -> ECR -> ECS with blue-green cutover. Infra/security constraints still apply (mTLS via Envoy, pool 20/instance, ALB 1MB payload, DR/backup policies).

At 1500 req/min from one IP: Gateway account-level limits (2000 steady, 5000 burst) do not throttle; ALB per-IP limit at 1000/min drops/throttles ~500/min before containers; Auth handles up to ~1000/min from that IP.

JWT signing-key dependencies: Auth Service token issuance (local private key), JWKS publication endpoint, Gateway Lambda authorizer validation, Portfolio Service validation, Notification Service validation, and any other downstream service that validates Meridian JWTs.

Commit-to-prod flow: code review (Claude Code before merge), then GitHub Actions runs lint -> typecheck -> test -> build -> push image to ECR -> deploy to ECS, followed by blue-green ALB target switch for zero-downtime rollout. Fargate Spot is reserved for batch workloads only.
